elements.sent-2411-001: Building pluralistic AI requires designing models that are able to be shaped to represent a wide range of value systems and cultures.
elements.sent-2411-002: Achieving this requires first being able to evaluate the degree to which a given model is capable of reflecting various personas.
elements.sent-2411-003: To this end, we propose a benchmark for evaluating the steerability of model personas as a function of prompting.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our design is based on a formal definition of prompt steerability, which analyzes the degree to which a model's joint behavioral distribution can be shifted from its baseline behavior.
elements.sent-2411-005: By defining steerability indices and inspecting how these indices change as a function of steering effort, we can estimate the steerability of a model across various persona dimensions and directions.
elements.sent-2411-006: Our benchmark reveals that the steerability of many current models is limited -- due to both a skew in their baseline behavior and an asymmetry in their steerability across many persona dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-007: We release an implementation of our benchmark at https://github.com/IBM/prompt-steering.
elements.sent-2411-008: A primary question underlying alignment research is: who are we are aligning to? The philosophy of AI/algorithmic pluralism \cite{klingefjord2024human,jain2024algorithmic,sorensen2024value,sorensen2024roadmap states that we should design AI systems such that they are capable of representing various individuals/groups, rather than aligning to a single ``average'' human preference -- a practice that is unfortunately common in many current model training pipelines.}
elements.sent-2411-009: One mechanism for enabling pluralism is by constructing steerable models, i.e., models that can be (easily) made to adopt various behaviors \cite{sorensen2024roadmap .}
elements.sent-2411-010: In this paper, we propose a methodology for evaluating a model's steerability with respect to prompting.
elements.sent-2411-011: We first propose a formal definition for prompt steerability -- quantifying a model's behavior as a joint distribution, which we term a profile, computed via evaluation/score functions on the distribution of model generations as a result of (a set of) input prompts.
elements.sent-2411-012: Using a dataset of model personas \cite{perez2022discovering , we design a benchmark that measures the extent to which a model can be prompted to adopt various personas.}
elements.sent-2411-013: Furthermore, building on our definition of prompt steerability, we define steerability indices that enable comparative measures of how much a model's behavior can be influenced.
elements.sent-2411-014: While there are a (growing) number of methods for steering models -- via prompting \cite{bhargava2023s, li2023steerability, li2024guiding , fine-tuning \cite{perez2022discovering, alves2023steering , activations \cite{rimsky2023steering, turner2023activation, stickland2024steering, lee2024programming , and other methods \cite{huang2024deal, gu2024chared, han2024word -- prompting is one of the most straightforward ways in which a typical user can influence a model's behavior.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: Often it is not feasible for a user to fine-tune a model (either due to computational requirements or simply due to not having access to the weights) or steer a model via its activations (which requires being able to access/modify a model's internals during inference).. {\bf Related work.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Steerability is a closely related notion to model alignment, with much of the community treating steering and aligning as interchangeable concepts.
elements.sent-2411-017: We emphasize, however, that the notion of steerability describes the extent to which a model can be aligned/steered along a given dimension.
elements.sent-2411-018: Some models can be aligned to a specific behavior more readily than others -- this is precisely what steerability aims to quantify.
elements.sent-2411-019: There is a variety of recent research concerning steerability, ranging from theoretical to practical.
elements.sent-2411-020: Perhaps most prominent of the theoretical results is that of \cite{wolf2023fundamental in which the authors present an existence theorem stating that, under the assumption that LLMs perform Bayesian inference, there exists a prompt that can amplify any existing model behavior.}
elements.sent-2411-021: It is worth emphasizing that the authors do not describe what this prompt looks like nor prescribe how to find this prompt, simply that it exists.
elements.sent-2411-022: Similar theoretical work \cite{bhargava2023s finds that there exist short prompt sequences that can significantly alter the probability of specific output tokens.}
elements.sent-2411-023: On the practical side, many recent papers propose algorithms for steering models to specific behaviors \cite{zhou2022steering, perez2022discovering, rimsky2023steering, li2023steerability, turner2023activation, li2024guiding .}
elements.sent-2411-024: Of the algorithmic papers, that of \cite{perez2022discovering is most relevant to the present paper, with the fundamental difference being that the authors explore steerability with respect to fine-tuning (specifically via RL from human feedback) where our methodology studies prompting.}
elements.sent-2411-025: Lastly, model steerability is related to the notion of model sycophancy \cite{ranaldi2023large, sharma2023towards, malik2024sycophancy with the primary difference being that the latter studies the degree to which the models mirror input biases in their outputs.. {\bf Contribution.}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Our primary contribution is the development of a steerability benchmark for evaluating the degree to which a model can be prompted to take on various personas.
elements.sent-2411-027: We additionally introduce metrics, termed steerability indices, to quantify the degree of steering.
elements.sent-2411-028: Our results complement the fine-tuning setting of \cite{perez2022discovering by analyzing steerability of model personas via prompting.}
elements.sent-2411-029: We first define what we mean by prompt steerability.
elements.sent-2411-030: Given a generative language model $M_\theta$, where $\theta$ is the set of model parameters, denote $p_\theta$ as the probabilistic function that maps inputs/prompts $x\in \Xs$ to outputs $y\in \Ys$ via $y\sim p_\theta(x)$.
elements.sent-2411-031: Let $\Ss = \{s_1,\ldots,s_n\}$ denote a set of score functions, i.e., metrics, where each $s_i\in\Ss$ is a probabilistic function $s_i:\Xs\times \Ys\to P(\Es_i)$ from prompt-output pairs $(x,y)$ to a score in an evaluation space $\Es_i\subseteq \Rbb$, i.e., the values that score $s_i$ can take.
elements.sent-2411-032: The score functions $\Ss$, along with a set of prompts $X\subseteq\Xs$, yield a measure of a given language model's outputs, termed an evaluation profile.
elements.sent-2411-033: Formally, an evaluation profile is a joint distribution $\pbm_{X}\in\Ps = P(\Esbm)$, $\Esbm=\Es_1\times\cdots\times \Es_n$, defined as \begin{equation}\label{eq:base_profile} \pbm_{X} = \Ebb\big[p\big(\sbm(x,y)\big)\mid y\sim p_\theta(x), x\in X\big]\end{equation} where $p(\sbm(x,y))$ is the joint distribution of scores $\sbm(x,y) = (s_1(x,y), \ldots, s_n(x,y))$ for a given $(x,y)$ pair.
elements.sent-2411-034: In other words, a model's evaluation profile (or simply profile) $\pbm_X$ is the model's expected behavior on $X$ as measured by the score functions $\Ss$.
elements.sent-2411-035: A model's prompt steerability measures the degree to which the model's profile changes, as a function of prompting, along a set of steering dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-036: Define a prompt steering function $\sigma:X\to\Xs$ as a function that generates modified prompts that influence the model's outputs via $y\sim p_\theta(\sigma(x))$.
elements.sent-2411-037: Let \smash{$\Ds=\{d_1,\ldots,d_m\}$} denote the set of steering dimensions and define \smash{$\sigma_i^+$} (resp.
elements.sent-2411-038: For example, directing a model to respond in a more positive or negative tone could be achieved by defining steering functions $(\sigma_i^+, \sigma_i^-)$ that appropriately modify the model's system prompt.
elements.sent-2411-039: Define the positively and negatively steered profiles along $d_i$ as \begin{equation}\pbm_{X}^{i+} = \Ebb\big[p\big(\sbm(x',y)\big)\mid y\sim p_\theta(x'), x'=\sigma_i^+(x), x\in X\big]\label{eq:pos_profile} \pbm_{X}^{i-} = \Ebb\big[p\big(\sbm(x',y)\big)\mid y\sim p_\theta(x'), x'=\sigma_i^-(x), x\in X\big]\label{eq:neg_profile}\end{equation} A model's prompt steerability along $d_i$ is the degree to which $(\pbm_{X ^{i+ ,\pbm_{X ^{i- )$ can be pulled away from $\pbm_X$ by construction of \smash{$(\sigma_i^+,\sigma_i^-)$}.}}}}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\pbm_{X}^{i+} = \Ebb\big[p\big(\sbm(x',y)\big)\mid y\sim p_\theta(x'), x'=\sigma_i^+(x), x\in X\big]\label{eq:pos_profile} \pbm_{X}^{i-} = \Ebb\big[p\big(\sbm(x',y)\big)\mid y\sim p_\theta(x'), x'=\sigma_i^-(x), x\in X\big]\label{eq:neg_profile}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-040: Further quantification of a model's prompt steerability is dependent upon the specific setting, requiring a definition of both the precise steering functions as well as assigning an appropriate distance metric between profiles (distributions).
elements.sent-2411-041: We quantify these notions in the context of persona-based prompt steerability in the following section.
elements.sent-2411-042: Prompt steerability of model's persona describes the degree that a model can be made to adopt various personas by prompting alone.
elements.sent-2411-043: We design a benchmark that enables measurement of this property.
elements.sent-2411-044: Our benchmark is based on the evals/persona dataset\footnote{https://github.com/anthropics/evals/tree/main/persona which consists of model persona dimensions spanning personality, political views, ethical views, religious views, unsafe behaviors, and other topics \cite{perez2022discovering .}}
elements.sent-2411-045: The dataset contains multiple statements for each persona dimension (e.g., agreeableness, willingness-to-defer-to-experts, politically-liberal, etc.) and each direction (positive, negative).
elements.sent-2411-046: The statements are simple strings that are designed to align with a given persona dimension and direction (with their degree of alignment given by a label\_confidence parameter).
elements.sent-2411-047: Additional details on the data can be found in Appendix \ref{app:data_prep .. {\bf Methodology.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: Both the steering and scoring of a model's outputs are done via the persona statements.
elements.sent-2411-049: Specifically, by decomposing the prompt as $x=(x_{\text{sys ,x_{\text{usr )$, where $x_{\text{sys }$ is the system prompt and $x_{\text{usr }$ is the user message, steering functions \smash{$(\sigma_{i,k ^+,\sigma_{i,k ^-)$} operate on the system prompt only, that is, \smash{$\sigma_{i,k ^+(x) = (\sigma_{i,k ^+(x_{\text{sys ),x_{\text{usr )$}, where \smash{$\sigma_{i,k ^+(x_{\text{sys )$} appends $k$ steering statements (i.e., principles) to the system prompt.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-050: Scoring of the model's outputs is done by asking the model (in the user message, $x_{\text{usr }$) if it would generate a given statement.}
elements.sent-2411-052: By comparing the model's (yes/no) answers to the user prompt with the statement's true direction (and label confidence), we can construct estimates of the steered profiles (see Appendix \ref{app:personas for details).}

elements.sent-2411-053: Note that because the model is both steered and scored using persona statements, the steering and scoring dimensions coincide ($m = n$).
elements.sent-2411-054: Also note that each statement is contained within a single persona dimension split, i.e., a given statement is only labeled with respect to a single persona dimension.
elements.sent-2411-055: Thus, when evaluating a model's answer, we can only reason about its behavior along its corresponding dimension, independently of other dimensions.

elements.sent-2411-058: The construction of the score functions in terms of the persona statements is detailed in Appendix \ref{app:personas .}
elements.sent-2411-059: Given the structure of the prompt steering function, we can further quantify the definition of prompt steerability.
elements.sent-2411-060: We define steerability indices $\big(\gamma_{i,k ^+, \gamma_{i,k ^-\big)$, \smash{$i\in[n]$}, \smash{$k\in\Nbb$}, as \begin{equation}\gamma_{i,k}^+ = \frac{W(p_X^i,\tilde p_{X}^{i+})-W(p_{X,k}^{i+},\tilde p_{X}^{i+})}{W(\tilde p_{X}^{i+}, \tilde p_{X}^{i-})}, \qquad\qquad \gamma_{i,k}^- = \frac{W(p_X^i,\tilde p_{X}^{i-}) - W(p_{X,k}^{i-},\tilde p_{X}^{i-})}{W(\tilde p_{X}^{i+}, \tilde p_{X}^{i-})}\end{equation} where \smash{$W(\cdot,\cdot)$} is the Wasserstein distance and \smash{$\tilde p_{X ^{i+}$}, resp.}}}

elements.sent-2411-061: Intuitively, the steerability indices describe the extent to which the model's profile was steered relative to how far it could have been steered, i.e., its steering capacity.

elements.sent-2411-063: As such, both \smash{$\gamma_{i,k ^+$} and \smash{$\gamma_{i,k ^-$} lie in $[-1,1]$.}}
elements.sent-2411-064: Plotting the steerability indices over $k$ yields steerability curves, i.e., the extent to which the model can be steered as a function of the steering effort (number of steering statements).
elements.sent-2411-065: Some steerability curves are shown in Fig. \ref{fig:steer_curves .}
elements.sent-2411-066: Generally, we observe that more steering examples yield a more steered model, with the resulting steered direction in agreement with the attempted steering direction.\footnote{Note that there are exceptions to this for some dimensions/models; see Appendix \ref{app:results .}}
elements.sent-2411-067: In particular, more advanced models tend to possess steerability curves that both yield higher values (higher degree of steering) and plateau sooner, indicating a greater ease of steering.
elements.sent-2411-068: This early flattening behavior is likely due to more sophisticated models having better internal representations, allowing them to infer what the user is asking of it from fewer examples.
elements.sent-2411-069: While larger models are generally more steerable than smaller models, the limited extent to which (even current SoTA) models can be steered poses various challenges for building pluralistic AI.
elements.sent-2411-070: A model's steerability, as computed by its steerability indices, is necessarily relative to its base behavior.
elements.sent-2411-071: As shown in Appendix \ref{app:results , many model's unsteered (baseline) behavior across various dimensions is not centered around a neutral point.}
elements.sent-2411-072: Additionally, the steerability from this baseline is often asymmetric, with models generally able to be steered more easily in one direction than the other.
elements.sent-2411-073: For instance, as shown in Fig. \ref{fig:steer_curves , many of the models we benchmarked were able to be steered more in the negative direction than the positive direction of the dimension: subscribes-to-utilitarianism.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Similar asymmetries exist for many of the other dimensions we studied.
elements.sent-2411-075: Notably, many models were more easily steered in the negative direction than the positive direction, with some resisting positive steering on some dimensions altogether.
elements.sent-2411-076: Appendix \ref{app:results provides detailed benchmark results for a collection of models, namely: llama-3-8b-instruct, llama-3.1-8b-instruct, granite-7b-lab, granite-13b-chat-v2, phi-3-mini-4k-instruct, and phi-3-medium-4k-instruct.}
elements.sent-2411-077: These results indicate that models possess internal baseline personas that are steerable, but noticeably resistant to steering along some dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-078: This rigidity limits a model's behavior to a constrained region, preventing models from adopting the range of personas necessary for a fully pluralistic AI.
elements.sent-2411-079: We present an experimental methodology for evaluating a model's steerability with respect to prompting.
elements.sent-2411-080: We first constructed a principled definition of a model's prompt steerability and, using this definition, we designed a benchmark for evaluating a model's steerability across various personas.
elements.sent-2411-081: We observed that many models resist steering on various dimensions/directions indicating that models possess (rigid) internal personas.
elements.sent-2411-082: Despite the limited steerability of many current models, our benchmark provides an approach to evaluate the steerability of models, providing a signal to design models that are more steerable.
elements.sent-2411-083: Current efforts are focused on better understanding the underlying reasons why some models are more steerable than others, with the goal of enabling {controllable generation for the design of pluralistic AI systems.}

elements.sent-2411-086: Limitations of our current benchmark design concern efficiency (the number of model calls may be high when considering a large set of dimensions) and the inability to study joint steerability (as mentioned earlier, the nature of the dataset only allows for studying steerability along individual dimensions).
elements.sent-2411-087: Additionally, our approach heavily depends on the quality of the source dataset (in this case the persona statements) and the completeness of the prompt set $X$.
elements.sent-2411-088: Statements that do not accurately reflect the intended dimensions or profiling using an overly sparse prompt set $X$ can lead to an incomplete view of model behavior.
elements.sent-2411-089: Relatedly, we are cognizant of the possibility that the benchmark results may only be an approximation for how a model would behave in reality (due to various reasons including specific phrasing or word choice in the persona statements, or the possibility that yes/no answers are an approximate measure of how a model actually behaves, e.g., in free-form outputs).
elements.sent-2411-090: Caricature effects \cite{cheng2023marked are also an important consideration that have not been studied in the current paper (diversifying the set of persona statements may be an effective method to combat these effects).}
elements.sent-2411-091: Lastly, it is worth pointing out that the method we use for steering is reminiscent of the many-shot jailbreaking (MSJ) attack \cite{anil2024many .}
elements.sent-2411-092: If a model has a mitigation mechanism for MSJ attacks, it may also resist system prompt steering.
elements.sent-2411-093: Understanding the steerability of LLMs is central to understanding their risk.
elements.sent-2411-094: While more steerable models are able to more easily be induced to reflect certain behavior, this behavior need not be good, i.e., asking the model to validate an incorrect or harmful view.
elements.sent-2411-095: While there is a risk of informing malicious actors which models are more able to be steered in certain directions, we feel that there is value in being transparent about which models are more easily influenced via prompting.
elements.section-2411-007: Prompt Steerability of Personas: Experiment Design
elements.sent-2411-096: The persona steerability benchmarking experiment is based on Anthropic's evals/persona dataset.\footnote{https://github.com/anthropics/evals/tree/main/persona .}
elements.sent-2411-097: The evals/persona dataset consists of 133 dimensions spanning personality, political views, ethical views, religious views, unsafe behaviors, and other topics \cite{perez2022discovering .}
elements.sent-2411-098: Each dimension consists of 500 examples (statements) in each of the positive and negative direction (1000 examples total per dimension).
elements.sent-2411-099: Associated with each example is a label confidence, in $[0.5,1]$, indicating the expected accuracy of the label (given by a preference model) for the current dimension and direction.
elements.sent-2411-100: Additional details of how these examples were constructed can be found in the original paper \cite{perez2022discovering .}
elements.sent-2411-101: For the purposes of the benchmark, we filter the original dimensions based on a desired minimum number of sufficiently confident examples.
elements.sent-2411-102: We set a minimum confidence threshold of $0.85$ and a minimum count of $300$ examples in each direction.
elements.sent-2411-103: Dimensions that possess more than $300$ examples are pruned down to $300$.
elements.sent-2411-104: Filtering based on these requirements yields a pruned set of examples across 70 dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-105: For computational reasons, we further prune this set down to the 32 dimensions listed in Fig. \ref{fig:dims .}
elements.sent-2411-106: Some example statements from this data are illustrated in Table \ref{table:data .}
elements.sent-2411-107: We split this data into 100 steering examples and 200 profiling examples for each direction and dimension.

elements.sent-2411-108: The specific benchmark procedure naturally fits into the formalism for prompt steering described in Sec.. \ref{sec:prompt_steering .}
elements.sent-2411-109: Specifically, for each persona dimension $d_i$, let the score $s_i(x,y)$ represent the probability that the model's response $y$ exhibits the positive valence of persona dimension $d_i$ in response to the prompt $x$.
elements.sent-2411-110: By collecting answers\footnote{The specific procedure for how answers are extracted from the model is given in Appendix \ref{app:extracting .}}
elements.sent-2411-111: This estimate is precisely the model's profile.
elements.sent-2411-112: The construction of the model's profile from the collected answer-valence pairs (response data) is described in the following subsection.
elements.sent-2411-113: Given that each score function $s_i$ represents a probability, scores are estimated via a beta distribution.
elements.sent-2411-114: Let $\{(a_x,v_x): x\in X\}$ represent the response data under $X$ where $a_x = answer[x]$ is the model's answer to $x=(x_{\text{sys ,x_{\text{usr )$ and $v_x = valence[x]\in\{+,-\}$ is the valence of $x$ (defined by the valence of the profiling statement in $x_\text{usr}$).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-115: Beta distributions are formed by comparing the model's answer with the question's valence and updating the posterior as a function of the profiling statement's label confidence.
elements.sent-2411-116: Define the belief increment $\delta_x = 2(c_x-0.5)$ where $c_x$ represents the label\_confidence of the profiling statement $x$, i.e., minimal confidence ($c_x=0.5$) will yield a zero increment.
elements.sent-2411-117: If $(a_x,v_x)\in\{(yes, +), (no, -)\}$ then $\alpha$ is incremented by $\delta_x$ with $\beta$ unchanged, whereas if $(a_x,v_x)\in\{(no, +), (yes, -)\}$ then $\beta$ is incremented by $\delta_x$ with $\alpha$ unchanged.
elements.sent-2411-118: This procedure applies for constructing both the unsteered (baseline) and steered profile estimates.. {\bf Benchmark parameters.}
elements.sent-2411-119: In general, evaluating a model's steerability is inherently a distributional exercise in the sense that we aim to characterize the extent to which profiles (joint distributions) change as a function of the steering mechanism (namely prompting in the current paper).
elements.sent-2411-120: As such, the evaluation requires simulating multiple samples of steering and scoring/profiling.
elements.sent-2411-121: Our benchmark requires various sampling parameters to be set: number of experiment trials, $T_e$; number of profiling statements, $N_p$, steering budget, $k$, and number of steering trials, $T_s$.
elements.sent-2411-122: These parameters help to control for both the specific elements and order of the steering statements (in the system prompt) and the profiling questions.
elements.sent-2411-123: Note for models that do not natively have system prompts, we prepend the system prompt to the user query (following standard practice).
elements.sent-2411-124: Our benchmark design requires that we are able to extract a model's yes/no response to the profiling question.
elements.sent-2411-125: We implement two approaches for this.. {\bf Output parsing.}
elements.sent-2411-126: Format instructions (as a JSON) are appended to the prompt.
elements.sent-2411-127: The output is then parsed to extract the desired key-value pairs.
elements.sent-2411-128: Given the imperfect instruction following rate of models, this method usually requires multiple calls before all outputs can be successfully parsed and thus can be inefficient (if the model does not follow instructions).. {\bf Comparing logprobs.}
elements.sent-2411-129: Each of the yes/no completions is appended to a given input prompt (see Fig. \ref{fig:prompt_template ) and each passed into the model.}
elements.sent-2411-130: By comparing the logprobs of the completion token (similar to the method of constrained decoding), we can infer which answer the model prefers to the given question.
elements.sent-2411-131: This process is very efficient but requires that the API provides access to the model's logprobs.

elements.sent-2411-132: Our design is based on a formal definition of prompt steerability, which analyzes the degree to which a model's joint behavioral distribution can be shifted from its baseline.
elements.sent-2411-133: Our benchmark reveals that the steerability of many current models is limited --- due to both a skew in their baseline behavior and an asymmetry in their steerability across many persona dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-134: We release an implementation of our benchmark at \url{https://github.com/IBM/prompt-steering .}
elements.sent-2411-135: A primary question underlying alignment research is: whose views are we are aligning to? The philosophy of AI/algorithmic pluralism \cite{klingefjord2024human,jain2024algorithmic,sorensen2024value,sorensen2024roadmap, conitzer2024social states that we should design AI systems such that they are capable of representing various individuals/groups, rather than aligning to a single ``average'' human preference \cite{feng2024modular --- a common practice in many current model training pipelines.}}
elements.sent-2411-136: A necessary step in the construction of such models is being able to evaluate how much a model can be steered along a given dimension.
elements.sent-2411-137: This analysis inherently depends on the method used to steer the model, i.e., prompting \cite{lester2021power, zhou2022steering, bhargava2023s, li2023steerability, li2024guiding, he2024cos , fine-tuning \cite{perez2022discovering, alves2023steering, kowsher2024propulsion , activations \cite{rimsky2023steering, turner2023activation, stickland2024steering, lee2024programming , and others \cite{huang2024deal, gu2024chared, han2024word, chen2024pad .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-138: Our investigation focuses on prompting, primarily due to its simplicity in modifying model behavior.
elements.sent-2411-139: While fine-tuning/retraining and activation steering are generally more effective methods for influencing model behavior than prompting \cite{alves2023steering , it is often not feasible for a user to fine-tune a model (either due to computational requirements or simply due to lack of access to weights) or steer a model via its activations (which requires being able to access/modify a model's internals).}
elements.sent-2411-140: In this paper, we study the prompt steerability of models, i.e., the extent to which a model can be steered via prompting alone.
elements.sent-2411-141: Our analysis is based on the construction of an evaluation profile --- a joint distribution of model behavior computed via evaluation/score functions on the distribution of model generations in response to (a set of) input prompts.
elements.sent-2411-142: Using a dataset of model personas \cite{perez2022discovering , we design a benchmark that measures the extent to which a model's profile can be prompted to reflect various personas.}
elements.sent-2411-143: Importantly, our analysis focuses on steerability on multiple dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-144: This is in contrast with much of alignment research that focuses on designing a single reward/preference model as the alignment objective.
elements.sent-2411-145: We define steerability indices that enable comparative measures of how much a model's profile changes on each dimension as a result of the number of steering statements (i.e., prompting effort).
elements.sent-2411-146: We additionally provide a visualization of model steerability, via steerability curves, which illustrate how model behavior (as described by the indices) changes as a function of prompting effort.
elements.sent-2411-147: Some models can be aligned to a specific behavior more readily than others --- this is precisely what steerability aims to quantify.
elements.sent-2411-148: While there are a significant (and growing) number of papers that propose methods for aligning models \cite{zhou2022steering, perez2022discovering, rimsky2023steering, li2023steerability, turner2023activation, li2024guiding , there are far fewer that study how easily models can be aligned, i.e., steerability.}
elements.sent-2411-149: Focusing on steerability with respect to prompting, relevant theoretical works \cite{wolf2023fundamental, bhargava2023s concern the existence of prompts that can modify behavior.}
elements.sent-2411-150: Namely, \cite{wolf2023fundamental presents an existence theorem stating that, under the assumption that LLMs perform Bayesian inference, there exists a prompt that can amplify any existing model behavior.}
elements.sent-2411-151: The work of \cite{bhargava2023s adopts a control theoretic perspective and finds that there exist short prompt sequences that can significantly alter the probability of specific output tokens.}
elements.sent-2411-152: Notably, \cite{wolf2023fundamental doesn't prescribe how to find this prompt, simply that it exists, and neither \cite{wolf2023fundamental nor \cite{bhargava2023s describe its form.}}}
elements.sent-2411-153: More practical investigations of prompt steerability focus on a model's ability to adopt various personas.
elements.sent-2411-154: The work of \cite{li2023steerability uses persona data, namely the OpinionsQA dataset \cite{santurkar2023whose , to learn embeddings of various personas, from which soft-prompts are designed (via prompt tuning) to steer model behavior to specific personas.}}
elements.sent-2411-155: Other work \cite{liu2024evaluating studies model steerability of ``incongruous'' personas (personas that contain statistically unlikely combinations of traits), also derived from OpinionsQA, and find that steerability to incongruous personas is lower than to congruous personas.}
elements.sent-2411-156: More recently, the work of \cite{samuel2024personagym introduces a framework that allows for evaluation of how much a model reflects a given (multidimensional) persona.}
elements.sent-2411-157: In contrast to the above papers, the present paper focuses on benchmarking model steerability.
elements.sent-2411-158: This differs from the focus of \cite{li2023steerability --- concerning the development of a steering method (using prompt tuning) --- and \cite{liu2024evaluating --- who primarily focus on studying model bias via steerability.}}
elements.sent-2411-159: Lastly, the work of \cite{samuel2024personagym focuses on evaluating a given ``persona agent'' rather than evaluating the degree to which a model can be steered to adopt different personas.. {\bf Moral surveying.}}
elements.sent-2411-160: There is a growing body of research around gaining insights into the ``morals'' of language models by analyzing their responses to psychological surveys designed for humans.
elements.sent-2411-161: Namely \cite{yao2023value use Schwartz’s theory of basic values \cite{schwartz2012overview to construct a dataset (termed FULCRA) to evaluate how a model's behavior maps to the basic values.}}
elements.sent-2411-162: Other work, \cite{abdulhai2023moral and \cite{tlaie2024exploring , use the moral foundations questionnaire \cite{graham2008moral , to analyze which morals are most expressed in the language model's outputs.}}}

elements.sent-2411-165: The prompt steerability of a model is related to how well a model can learn from in-context examples \cite{brown2020language,wies2024learnability .}
elements.sent-2411-166: Early work \cite{brown2020language empirically studied this property by constructing ``in-context learning curves'', observing that ``large models demonstrate improved ability to learn a task from contextual information.'' More recent results provide further evidence for this observation \cite{wei2023larger .}}
elements.sent-2411-167: The results of the present paper, namely that more sophisticated models tend to possess a higher steerability, appears to align with this observation, signaling at a potential connection between the two properties.
elements.sent-2411-168: The primary contributions of our paper are:.
elements.sent-2411-169: Development of a prompt steerability benchmark: We design (and open-source) a benchmark to evaluate the degree to which a model can be prompted to take on various personas.
elements.sent-2411-170: To the best of our knowledge, this is the first benchmark that measures the steerability of models with respect to prompting.
elements.sent-2411-171: Uniform comparison of steerability: We introduce metrics, termed steerability indices, to quantify the degree of prompt steering.
elements.sent-2411-172: Importantly, the indices take into account each model's baseline (unsteered) performance, allowing for a uniform comparison of prompt steerability across models and dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-173: Our results complement the fine-tuning setting of \cite{perez2022discovering , with the fundamental difference being that \cite{perez2022discovering explores steerability with respect to fine-tuning (specifically via RL from human feedback) whereas our methodology studies prompting.}}
elements.sent-2411-174: To facilitate our analysis of prompt steerability, we introduce the notion of an evaluation profile --- a formal description of a model's behavior on a given set of inputs as measured by a set of score functions.
elements.sent-2411-175: A model's behavior, given by its profile, can be influenced (or steered) via a prompt steering function.
elements.sent-2411-176: We define both of these objects below.. {\bf Evaluation profiles.}
elements.sent-2411-177: Note that score functions are stochastic, i.e., a score function, in general, generates a distribution of scores for a given input-output pair $(x,y)\in \Xs\times \Ys$.\footnote{Such score functions arise in scenarios such as sampling-based metrics (e.g., using LLM-as-a-judge), context-dependent evaluations, or those derived from human evaluation datasets.}
elements.sent-2411-178: The definition of score functions also allows for deterministic (degenerate) score functions.
elements.sent-2411-179: The score functions $\Ss$, along with a set of prompts $X\subseteq\Xs$, termed profiling prompts, yield a measure of a given language model's outputs, termed an evaluation profile.
elements.sent-2411-180: Formally, an evaluation profile is a joint distribution $\pbm_X\in\Ps = P(\Esbm)$, $\Esbm=\Es_1\times\cdots\times \Es_n$, defined as \begin{equation}\label{eq:base_profile} \pbm_X = \Ebb\big[p\big(\sbm(x,y)\big)\mid y\sim p_\theta(x), x\in X \big]\end{equation} where $p(\sbm(x,y))$ is the joint distribution of scores $\sbm(x,y) = (s_1(x,y), \ldots, s_n(x,y))$ for a given $(x,y)$ pair.
elements.sent-2411-181: In other words, a model's evaluation profile (or simply profile) $\pbm_X$ is the model's expected behavior on $X$ as measured by $\Ss$.
elements.sent-2411-182: To simplify notation, we write $\pbm_X$ as $\pbm$ since $X$ is always a dependence.
elements.sent-2411-183: A model's profile can be influenced, or steered, by adapting the prompts that are fed into the model.
elements.sent-2411-184: This is done via a prompt steering function $\sigma:X\to P(\Xs)$ which maps a profiling prompt $x\in X$ to an adapted prompt (in $\Xs$).
elements.sent-2411-185: Given $\sigma$, the model's steered profile is given by \begin{equation}\nonumber\pbm_\sigma = \Ebb\big[p\big(\sbm(x',y)\big)\mid\, y\sim p_\theta(x'), \nonumber x'\sim\sigma(x), x\in X\big]\label{eq:steered_profile}\end{equation} where the modified prompt $x'$ is generated from $\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-186: The degree to which $\sigma$ has steered a model can be analyzed by comparing $\pbm$ and $\pbm_\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-187: Informally, a model's prompt steerability is the degree to which $\pbm_\sigma$ can be pulled away from $\pbm$ by construction of~$\sigma$.
elements.sent-2411-188: Prompt steerability of a model's persona describes the degree to which a model can be made to adopt different personas by prompting alone.
elements.sent-2411-189: We design a benchmark that enables measurement of this property .
elements.sent-2411-190: Persona data is sourced from Anthropic's persona dataset \cite{perez2022discovering .}
elements.sent-2411-191: The dataset contains statements for various persona dimensions, e.g., agreeableness, politically-liberal, ends-justify-means, etc. The statements are simple strings constructed to align with a given persona dimension and direction, for example, some sample statements for agreeableness are: \begin{equation}0.6\columnwidth{ ``It is important to treat other people with kindness and respect''} -\colorbox{lightgreen}{positive} [0.5em] 0.6\columnwidth{ ``I tend to enjoy getting into confrontations and arguments with others''} -\colorbox{lightred}{negative}\end{equation} The degree to which a dimension's statement aligns to a given direction is captured by a label\_confidence parameter.

elements.sent-2411-278: While there is certainly still much to be done in defining good design principles for pluralistic systems, understanding a model's (prompt) steerability is an important first step.
elements.sent-2411-279: We propose a benchmark for evaluating the prompt steerability of language models across various persona dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-280: Through construction of an evaluation profile (as a measure of model behavior) we quantify the prompt steerability of a model via steerability indices.
elements.sent-2411-281: Importantly, the steerability indices are defined with respect to a model's baseline (unsteered) behavior, allowing for a uniform comparison of steerability across models and dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-282: Our results indicate that models possess internal baseline personas that are steerable, but noticeably resistant to steering along some dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-283: This rigidity limits a model's behavior to a constrained region around the base profile, and consequently prevents models from adopting the range of personas necessary for representing a fully pluralistic AI.
elements.sent-2411-284: The focus of the present paper is on the evaluation of steerability as opposed to understanding the specific mechanism for why some models are more steerable than others.
elements.sent-2411-285: However, we believe that there is a meaningful relationship between the prompt steerability of a model and its in-context learning ability \cite{wies2024learnability .}
elements.sent-2411-286: Exploring this relationship is a key future direction.
elements.sent-2411-287: Limitations of our current benchmark design concern efficiency (the number of model calls may be high when considering a large set of dimensions), the inability to study joint steerability (the nature of the dataset only allows for studying steerability along individual dimensions), and steering via single prompts as opposed to a sequence of prompts, i.e., a multi-turn setting \cite{wu2024aligning, miehling2024language , where each prompt is contextualized with respect to existing turns.}
elements.sent-2411-288: Statements that do not accurately reflect the intended dimensions or profiling using an insufficiently rich prompt set $X$ can lead to an incomplete view of model behavior.
elements.sent-2411-289: Relatedly, we are cognizant of the possibility that the benchmark results may only be an approximation for how a model would behave in reality, e.g., due to specific phrasing or word choice in the persona statements, or the possibility that yes/no answers are only an approximate measure of how a model actually behaves, e.g., in free-form outputs.
elements.sent-2411-290: This latter issue points to a difficult trade-off.
elements.sent-2411-291: Letting the model generate open-ended text to the non-polar adaptation of the profiling question requires us to evaluate the natural text output for adherence to a given persona (which is a challenging task).
elements.sent-2411-292: Doing this in a scalable way, e.g., by using a natural language classifier or another LLM, introduces biases from the specific judgement model which are hard to separate from the actual evaluation/judgement.
elements.sent-2411-293: On the other hand, forcing structured (binary) responses may be a poor approximation of real model behavior \cite{wang2024my .}
elements.sent-2411-294: This may help to describe some of the non-monotonic behavior observed when steering some of the models (e.g., phi-3-medium-4k-instruct).. \onecolumn.

elements.sent-2411-295: Evaluating a model's steerability is inherently a distributional exercise in the sense that we aim to characterize the extent to which profiles (joint distributions) change as a function of the steering mechanism (namely prompting in the current paper).
elements.sent-2411-296: Our benchmark requires various sampling parameters to be set (see source code): number of experiment trials, $T_e$; number of profiling statements, $n_\text{prf}$, steering budget, $k$, and number of steering trials, $T_\text{str}$.
elements.sent-2411-297: Note for models that do not natively have system prompts, we prepend the system prompt to the user prompt (following standard practice).
elements.sent-2411-298: As outlined in the main body of the paper, our primary approach for this is by comparing logprobs.
elements.sent-2411-299: We additionally implement an alternative approach by parsing the output directly.
elements.sent-2411-300: A model's answer is extracted by comparing the model's logprobs for completions of yes versus No. Denote $l_x$ as the difference of logprobs, defined as \begin{equation}l_x:=logprob[\,yes\mid x] - logprob[\,no\mid x]\end{equation} where $\text{logprob [\,<answer>\mid x]$ represents the model's logprob of the <answer> token when appended to the base prompt/question $x$.}
elements.sent-2411-301: If $l_x\ge0$ then the model prefers yes over no in responding to question $x$ (and conversely for $l_x<0$).
elements.sent-2411-302: In other words, each of the yes/no completions is appended to a given input prompt (see Fig. \ref{fig:prompt_template ) and passed into the model.}
elements.sent-2411-303: This process is very efficient but requires that the API provides access to the model's logprobs.. {\bf Output parsing.}
elements.sent-2411-304: Format instructions (as a JSON) are appended to the prompt (via langchain response schemas).
elements.sent-2411-305: Given the imperfect instruction following rate of models, this method usually requires multiple calls before all outputs can be successfully parsed and thus can be inefficient (if the model does not follow instructions).
